segments.s070-g73: بسان + noun/adj is a simile: 'like/as a ...'. سپهری is an adjectival/nominal descriptor 'heavenly, celestial'. یکی is the numeral 'one/a single' placed before the noun. تخت زر is a classical compound meaning 'gold (زر) throne (تخت)' — note the ezāfe is dropped in poetic diction, so زر functions attributively (= 'golden throne').
segments.s070-g74: برو here is an archaic/poetic contraction of برِ او ('on it/upon it') rather than the common verb 'go' (no modern verb sense applies). ساخته is a past participle / perfect passive 'made' or 'set (there)'. چند گونه گهر is a noun group meaning 'several/kinds of jewels' where چند گونه = 'several kinds' modifies گهر; word order is noun phrase after the participle, typical of classical Persian descriptive sequences.
segments.s070-g75: This is a compact nominal phrase: برش is a noun here (contextually something like 'extent' or 'measure' — not the literal 'cut'), modified by خسروی 'royal'. بیست پهنای او is a numeric phrase where بیست 'twenty' quantifies پهنا/پهنای 'width/breadth' and او is the possessive pronoun 'its' ('twenty of its widths'). The clause is presented verbless here and completed by the following segment.
segments.s070-g76: چو here functions as 'as/so that' (classical connective). سیصد فزون literally 'three hundred more' — سیصد (three hundred) with فزون qualifying it as 'additional/more'. بود is the past of 'to be' (here 'there was / were'). بالای او = 'above it / atop it' (بالا + possessive او). The whole gives the result/state: 'and atop it were three hundred more.'
segments.s070-g77: وزان is a classical connective meaning 'and from those / of them'. ژنده‌پیلان is a poetic plural noun ('elephants' or giant beasts); هندی is an adjective 'Indian' following the noun (classical word order allows noun + adjective). The numeral چهار follows the noun phrase to indicate 'four' of those beasts.
segments.s070-g78: همه 'all' quantifies the following objects جامه و فرش 'garments and carpets'. کردند is the past-tense 3rd-person plural of کردن 'they did/made' used here in the sense 'they loaded / they carried' (کردند بار = 'they made/load(ed) a load'). بار functions as the object or adverbial 'load/ burden' placed after the verb in this classical word order.
segments.s070-g17: وزان پس (literally "from that then") is a compact poetic connective meaning "thereupon/from that moment." دوان is a participial/adverbial form meaning "rushing/springing forth." دست کرده is a perfective participial phrase "having put (his) hand" where دست ("hand") is the direct noun and کرده is the auxiliary/participle "having done/placed." به + کش here shows direction/target ("to the bow/at the pull"); the preposition به precedes the object, so the whole phrase describes the sudden action that immediately precedes the arrival in the next segment.
segments.s070-g18: بیامد is the past tense verb "came" used here as a simple narrative past. بر + شاه marks the goal/target ("upon/to the king"). خورشید فش is a proper-name phrase (compound epithet) following شاه; syntactically it is a noun phrase in apposition to "king," so the verb governs the whole phrase "came upon King Khurshidfash."
segments.s070-g19: بدو گفت is the common formulaic sequence "he said to him" (بدو = to him; گفت is the verb "said"); note that گفت here is the past verb, though the provided lemma senses list did not include the verb sense. بشنو is an imperative "listen/hear," and ز من یک سخن is a prepositional phrase "one word from me," with ز (poetic "from") preceding the speaker من and the noun سخن.
segments.s070-g20: چو here functions temporally/conditionally as "when/if." دیگر یکی کامت is an object phrase "another/one more desire of yours" (یکی = "one/a certain," دیگر = "other/another," کامت = "your desire" with the possessive suffix). آید is the present/subjunctive "comes/happens," and بکن is an imperative "do it" (the verb بکن follows the clause it responds to, a common pattern in Persian where the command finalizes the conditional advice).
segments.s070-g21: ترا را is the object phrase "you" (ترا with the object marker را separated in the given tokenization). خواسته‌گر is a noun "seeker/petitioner." ز بهر تنست is a classical locution meaning "is for the sake of the body" (ز بهر = "for/because of," تنست = "is [about] the body"), so the whole clause asserts the petitioner's motive is bodily/selfish.
segments.s070-g22: ببخش is an imperative meaning "forgive/let go." و is the coordinating conjunction "and." بدان is composed of the prefix ب (here the verbal prefix be-) plus دان ("know") forming the command/imperative "know/realize." کین شب آبستنست is a nominal clause "this night is pregnant with vengeance" where کین ("vengeance") is the substantive and آبستنست ("is pregnant") is a predicate adjective used figuratively; word order places the admonition (ببخش) before the warning (بدان ...).
segments.s070-g23: اگر چند باشد is a concessive clause "even if/although it be," with اگر introducing the concession and چند functioning here as a modifier meaning "though/although/somewhat long." باشد is the subjunctive/present form "be/may be." شب دیریاز (compound adjectival phrase) means "a long/long-drawn-out night." The clause sets up the contrast resolved in the following segment.
segments.s070-g24: برو is an imperative interjection here meaning "go away/be gone." تیرگی is the subject "darkness/gloom." هم نماند دراز is a verbless/result clause meaning "will not remain long" where هم adds emphasis "even/the same" and نماند is the present/future-oriented verb "remain/linger," followed by دراز ("long") as the duration adjective; together they mean the gloom will not last.
segments.s070-g49: مرا is the stressed/experiencer pronoun ('to me' / 'for me') placed before the rest of the clause. در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase ('in the world') modifying the predicate. انده is a participial/adjectival word here ('placed' / 'causing sorrow') that links to جان او (the noun phrase 'his life/soul'), and the clause closes with the copula ست ('is'), so the literal order is 'For me, in the world, (a) placed/causing thing — his life — is.'
segments.s070-g50: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb ('now'). با is the preposition 'with' and introduces the following noun (توم) as the accompanying party or instrument; روز پیمان is a nominal compound 'day‑(long) covenant' or 'a pledge for a day'. The clause ends again with the copula اوست ('it/he is'), so the structure reads 'Now, with X, the day's pledge is his.'
segments.s070-g51: ندارم is the negative present tense verb 'I do not have' (negated possession/experience). همی is an adverbial particle adding emphasis/continuity ('so/thus' or 'indeed'), and انده خویشتن is a nominalized/adjectival phrase ('such a placed/sustained thing for myself' — idiomatically 'such sorrow for myself'). The whole segment is an elliptical clause meaning 'I do not have such (sorrow) myself.'
segments.s070-g52: ازویست is a fused/explanatory phrase equivalent to از وی است ('it is from him/her' or 'it proceeds from him'), placed before the noun phrase it identifies. این درد و اندوه من is the nominal phrase 'this pain and sorrow of mine' serving as the thing that 'is from him'. Word order in older/poetic Persian often permits the 'it is from X' element to appear before the identified subject.
segments.s070-g53: یکی here means 'someone' or 'a certain one' (indefinite subject). سخت modifies پیمان as an adverbial/adjectival intensifier ('a severe/firm pledge'). پیمان ستد is a verb phrase where ستد (took/exacted) governs پیمان (the pledge), and زو (poetic zā = از او) is the prepositional source 'from her'; نخسـت ('first') gives temporal ordering — 'first he exacted a stern pledge from her.'
segments.s070-g54: پس آنگه is a two-word discourse marker ('then; afterwards'). به + مردی here expresses manner or quality — 'in manliness' / 'as a man' — so به مردی ره چاره جست is 'he sought (جست, past of seeking) the way/means (ره چاره) in a manly fashion' or 'he sought a remedy as a man.' Note جست is the simple past tense 'sought'.
segments.s070-g55: بیاراست is a past transitive verb 'he adorned' (perfective). تن را is the definite object with را marking ('his/the body' as the thing adorned). به دیبا و زر lists the materials/instruments introduced by به ('with brocade and gold'). The verb takes the object before the instrumental phrase, typical word order in Persian.
segments.s070-g56: This coordinate prepositional phrase repeats به ('with') for emphasis: به در و به یاقوت ('with pearls and with rubies'). پرمایه is an adjective 'rich/abundant' modifying سر ('head'), so پرمایه سر means 'a head abundant (with gems)'. The phrase therefore describes ornamentation of the head with pearls and rubies.
segments.s070-g1: چو is the poetic temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. در کابل is a locative prepositional phrase 'in Kabul' placed before the rest of the clause (typical Persian word order: preposition + place). این داستان فاش گشت is a past intransitive predicate: فاش گشت = 'became revealed/was made known' (گشت as an intransitive past auxiliary meaning 'became').
segments.s070-g2: سر مرزبان is a nominal phrase where سر ('head' / metonymically 'the person' or 'mind/temper' of the marzban) identifies whose state is described. پر ز + noun is an adjectival phrase 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'from/because of'), so پر ز پرخاش گشت means 'became full of reproach' with گشت again marking the change of state 'became'.
segments.s070-g3: برآشفت is a perfective/past verb 'became angry/was enraged'. و simply links the two sequential actions. سیندخت را پیش خواند is a past transitive clause: را marks the definite object (Sindokht), and پیش‌خواند (خواند with the adverbial پیش) = 'summoned/called forward' (past tense).
segments.s070-g4: همه خشم رودابه is a genitive/possessive sequence meaning 'the whole anger of Rudabeh' (همه = 'whole, entire' modifying خشم). بر وی is a prepositional phrase 'upon him/against him' (بر = 'on/upon', وی = 'him/her/it'), and براند is the classical narrative verb 'turned/drove' (here best read as past/narrative sense 'directed/placed' Rudabeh's wrath upon him).
segments.s070-g5: بدو گفت is the common construction 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', dative; گفت here is the narrative past of 'say'). کاکنون = 'now, at present' frames the time; جزین رای نیست is an idiomatic negation 'there is no counsel/plan except this' (جز = 'except', این = 'this', رای = 'counsel/plan', نیست = 'is not').
segments.s070-g6: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. با شاه گیتی is a circumstantial phrase 'with the king of the world' (با = 'with', شاه گیتی = 'king of the world'). مرا پای نیست is an idiomatic existential statement: مرا = 'for me' (dative/possessive 'to me'), پای = 'foot/standing/place' (here 'standing' or 'claim'), نیست = 'there is not/there is no' → 'I have no standing/claim.' Note: the tokenization provided splits مرا into م + را; treat them together as the pronoun 'مرا'.
segments.s070-g7: که again introduces a subordinate clause of intention/consequence. آرمت here is an archaic/poetic first‑person verbal form expressing the speaker's intention ('that I may/could ...'); followed by با دخت ناپاک تن — با = 'with' (here marking accompaniment/association), دخت ناپاک = 'the impure/unclean maiden', and تن = 'body' (object phrase 'the body of the impure maiden'). In context this phrase expresses what the speaker proposes to do with that body.
segments.s070-g8: کشم is a first‑person-form expressing intention/action 'I will pull/drag/kill' (classical/poetic verb form; read here as 'I shall cause' or 'I will bring about'). زارتان is built on زار = 'lament/wail' with the 2nd‑person plural suffix -تان → 'your lament' or 'cause you to wail'. بر سر انجمن is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of the assembly' (بر سر = 'upon/on the head of' → 'in front of', انجمن = 'assembly/meeting'). The whole predicate therefore expresses causing public shame/grief before the assembly.
segments.s070-g25: The first 'شود' (becomes) is the main verb placed before its subject 'روز' (day) — a common inverted/poetic word order. 'چون' introduces a simile ('like/as'), and the subordinate clause 'چشمه روشن شود' repeats the verb 'شود' (subordinate present/subjunctive sense 'becomes') describing how the day shines (i.e. 'as a spring becomes bright').
segments.s070-g26: 'جهان' is the subject 'the world'; 'چون' again makes a comparison ('like'); 'نگین بدخشان' is a noun phrase 'a gem of Badakhshan' where 'بدخشان' is adjectival/genitive 'of Badakhshan'; the final 'شود' is the verb 'becomes' (present/subjunctive/poetic).
segments.s070-g27: 'بدو گفت مهراب' is inverted word order: 'بدو' = 'to him/unto him' (dative) placed before the speech verb; 'گفت' is the past tense 'said' (lexical sense not listed in the supplied senses); 'مهراب' here is the speaker's name (a proper name rather than the architectural meaning); 'کز باستان' begins a temporal/relative phrase 'who/from ancient times' (here: 'from of old').
segments.s070-g28: 'مزن' is a negative imperative 'do not strike / do not tell' — here part of the idiom 'داستان زدن' = 'to tell a tale' so it means 'do not tell.' 'در میان یلان' is a prepositional phrase 'in/among the heroes' (در + میان + یلان), and 'داستان' is the object 'tale/ story.'
segments.s070-g29: 'بگو' is the second-person imperative 'say/tell.' 'آنچه دانی' is a relative clause 'that which you know' with 'دانی' = present 'you know' serving as the content of 'say.' 'جان را بکوش' uses 'را' to mark 'جان' as the direct object and the verb 'بکوش' (imperative 'strive/endeavor') — idiomatically 'risk/strain your life' = 'do your utmost even at the cost of your life.'
segments.s070-g30: 'وگر' is the conditional 'if / but if.' 'چادر خون' is a noun phrase 'a cloak/veil of blood' (چادر = cloak/veil). 'به تن' ('to/onto the body') uses the preposition 'به' plus the possessed noun 'تن' with the possessive suffix on 'خونم' in the next segment structure in the poem; here 'بر بپوش' is the compound imperative 'put on / don' with the prefix 'بر-' ('upon') and the imperative 'بپوش.' Altogether: 'If necessary, put on a blood-stained cloak over your body.'
segments.s070-g31: As before, 'بدو' is the dative 'to him'; 'گفت' is the past 'said' (sense not listed in the supplied options); 'سیندخت' is the speaker's name; 'کای' is the vocative particle 'O'; and 'سرفراز' is an epithet 'proud/exalted' addressing the addressee.
segments.s070-g32: 'بود' functions here as an existential/auxiliary 'there is / it is' introducing the noun 'کت' (coat). 'به خونم' is the prepositional phrase 'with my blood' (خون + possessive -م). 'نیاید نیاز' is an idiomatic negated construction: 'نیاید' ('should not come / does not arise') + 'نیاز' ('need') = 'no need arises,' i.e. 'it is not necessary.' Together: 'there is no need that the coat be stained with my blood.'
segments.s070-g57: This prepositional phrase strings several markers together: پس (here: 'then') begins the sequence; از is the ordinary preposition 'from/out of' taking گنج 'treasure' as its object; ز (poetic variant of از) repeats the 'from/of' relation and attaches to زرش 'his gold' (a genitive construction without an explicit ezâfe). بهر نثار is a purpose phrase ('for the sake of n-sâr / for offering') with بهر as a purposive preposition and نثار as the noun 'offering'. The whole phrase sets up source and purpose: 'then from the treasure — his gold — for offering...'.
segments.s070-g58: برون کرد is a two-word compound where برون (adverb) 'out/forth' precedes the past verb کرد 'did/made' and together mean 'took out / produced'. دینار is the direct object (marked by word order rather than را). چون before a numeral here works as a particle introducing the quantity 'as / amounting to' (i.e. 'to the tune of / namely'), followed by the number سی‌هزار 'thirty thousand'.
segments.s070-g59: به begins the phrase as a directional/benefactive preposition ('with' / 'in'), followed by زرین, an adjective 'golden', which directly modifies the following noun ستام. آوریدند is the past plural of آوردن 'they brought' (3rd-person plural perfect). The numeral سی follows the verb and is the quantity 'thirty' that they brought. Word order here is verb + object(s) with the adjectival modifier preceding the noun.
segments.s070-g60: This short prepositional phrase lists the sources: از + اسپان ('from horses/steeds'). تازی here qualifies اسپان; in context it means 'Tazi' horses — i.e., Arabian/Arabian-bred horses — so تازی functions adjectivally. و links the two sources, then از پارسی repeats the 'from' + adjective 'Persian' (پارسی) to mean 'from Persian [horses]'.
segments.s070-g61: This clause is elliptical and compressed, as is common in poetry. ابا is problematic here (it can mean 'to refuse' in prose), but in this line it functions as an agent or heading (likely a name or an archaic/poetical verb sense) — the line then names the object طوق زرین 'a golden collar' followed by پرستنده 'the worshipper/attendant' and the verb شست 'washed' (past). Grammatically, پرستنده is a participial noun modifying the person who شست; شست is a simple past verb 'he washed'. The lack of an explicit ezâfe or object marker is typical of poetic compression.
segments.s070-g62: این جمله uses a fronted numeral + noun 'یکی جام زر' = 'one (a) golden cup' where زر modifies جام; the following 'هر یکی را' ('each one' + definite object marker را) makes 'each (person) (had) the one [cup] as object', and 'به دست' is a locative phrase 'in (the) hand'. In short: 'one golden cup — each one had it in (his) hand.' The verb 'had' is omitted and supplied by context (elliptical).
segments.s070-g63: پر here is the adjective 'full (of)' taking an ablative/genitive complement introduced by از — 'full of musk and camphor and ruby and gold'. The coordinating و links the list of contents. This is a standard Persian adjective + از + list construction meaning 'filled/packed with ...'.
segments.s070-g64: ز is the poetic preposition equivalent to از ('of / from'), here linking the material or ornament source. پیروزه is used as a noun 'turquoise' (a stone) and governs the following quantity expression چند چندی ('several, a number of') which modifies گهر 'gems/jewels'. The reduplicated چند چندی is an intensifying or distributive poetic expression meaning 'several / a number of'.
segments.s070-g65: چهل (numeral) precedes the noun جامه 'garments' and counts them. جامه دیبای is a noun + specifying fabric (دیبا = brocade/silk) placed after or alongside the noun as is common in Persian poetic enumerations. پیکر 'on the body' indicates the locus where the garments hang; به زر uses به in an instrumental/adverbial sense 'with/by gold' to indicate material/ornamentation. The verb is omitted (catalog style), so this phrase functions like a noun cluster ('forty brocade garments on the body, with gold').
segments.s070-g66: طرازش is طراز + possessive -ش 'its pattern/design' and acts as the nominal heading for what follows. همه گونه گونه is an intensified adjective phrase: همه 'all' + گونه گونه (reduplication) = 'of every kind/various kinds.' گهر 'jewel' is the head noun; the whole clause means 'its design with jewels of every kind.' Again the verb is omitted, typical of poetic inventorying.
segments.s070-g67: به زرین و سیمین uses به + two adjectives زرین 'golden' and سیمین 'silvery' to mark the material/finish ('made of / in gold and silver'); the prepositional use is adverbial here. دوصد تیغ is numeral + noun 'two hundred swords.' هند follows as a postmodifier indicating origin/association ('of India' or 'Indian'), a common poetic compact order.
segments.s070-g68: جزان is a contraction of جزِ آن ('of those / of which') functioning as a partitive marker here. سی 'thirty' is the counted portion; به + name marks the indirect object ('to Zahrāb'). داده is the past participle 'given'; پرند (literally 'bird/winged one') appears after داده as the recipient epithet or part of a name (poetic names and epithets frequently follow one another). The clause reads roughly 'of these thirty were given to Zahrāb (Parand).'
segments.s070-g69: صد + noun gives 'one hundred X.' Here صد اشتر is a counted group. همه 'all' emphasizes that every one has the following traits. ماده 'female' and the compound سرخ موی 'red‑haired' are postpositive modifiers describing the members of the group (a common Persian pattern: noun → modifiers). The phrase means 'one hundred Ashtar, all female and red‑haired.'
segments.s070-g70: Again صد + noun = 'one hundred ...'. همه بارکش 'all burden‑bearing' treats بارکش as an adjectival/participial description 'used for carrying loads.' راه جوی is a compound where جوی (here 'seeking, -seeker') combines with راه 'road' to mean 'road‑seeking' or 'that follows/threads the road' — an adjectival phrase describing suitability for travel. Put together: 'one hundred (Esters), all beasts of burden, road‑traversing.'
segments.s070-g71: یکی 'a certain/one' is the indefinite numeral before the noun تاج 'crown.' پرگوهر is a compound adjective 'full of gems / jeweled' following the noun, and شاهوار is an honorific adjective 'kingly / majestic' further qualifying the crown. The phrase is a short noun phrase listing a single splendid crown.
segments.s070-g72: This is a coordinated list introduced by (ابا) طوق and با یاره و گوشوار. Here طوق 'necklace/collar' is a listed object; با is the preposition 'with' repeated to coordinate items. یاره in this context means 'attendant/companion' (an attendant or attendant-ornament) and گوشوار is 'earring.' The particle form ا(ب)ا at the start is an archaic/poetic sequencing particle (appearing here as a rhythmic variant of با) used to introduce the list; the series is a simple 'with X and with Y and earring' enumeration.
segments.s070-g41: A simple noun list joined by the conjunction و (and). The head items are in direct (indefinite) form: 'پرستنده' (the worshipper/devotee) followed by 'اسپ' (steed/horse), 'تخت' (throne/platform) and 'کلاه' (hat). The series is meant as the objects to be brought; Persian commonly lists nouns with و between them without additional case marking.
segments.s070-g42: Imperative/command construction: 'بیارای' functions as a verb meaning 'make ready / array / prepare' addressed to the addressee (here glossed as an instruction). The conjunction و links this verb to the following prepositional phrase. 'با خویشتن' = 'with yourself/with them' (خویشتن emphatic reflexive), and the combination 'بر به راه' is a poetic sequence of prepositions meaning 'on / toward the road' (بر + به giving direction 'onto/toward the way').
segments.s070-g43: An conditional/exception clause introduced by مگر which here means 'unless'. 'شهر کابل' is the subject (the city of Kabul). 'نسوزد' is a present-subjunctive/negative‑form verb (ن‑ + سوزد) meaning 'burn' in a clause governed by مگر: 'unless the city of Kabul burn (for us)'. 'به ما' marks the beneficiary/experiencer 'for us'.
segments.s070-g44: Temporal clause introduced by چو = 'when'. 'پژمرده شد' is a past/result clause 'became withered' (شد as perfective auxiliary/become). The main clause verb 'برفروزد' is third‑person present/future‑like and here means 'it will kindle/flare up'; the line reads as a general temporal sequence: 'when it becomes withered, it will kindle (for us)'. 'به ما' again marks 'for us'.
segments.s070-g45: Fixed Reporting formula: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus said' (چنین = thus/so, گفت = said). The proper name 'سیندخت' is the subject. 'کای' here is a vocative/title marker (Kay) attached to the name that follows or addresses a royal addressee, and 'نامدار' is an epithet 'renowned'. Word order is Subject + epithet in poetic register.
segments.s070-g46: Short nominal phrase whose internal relations are compact and somewhat elliptical in poetry: 'به جای' = 'in place of / instead of'; 'روان' = 'soul/spirit' (likely the noun governed by 'به جای'); 'خواسته' is a participial/nominal form 'desired' or 'wish' (here nominalized), and 'خواردار' is an adjectival/epithet (literal: 'one who eats/consumer' or figurative). The sequence reads as a compact noun phrase showing replacement/ desire (literal order: 'in place of the soul, a desired [thing] — the eater/consumer'), a construction typical of compressed poetic description (some semantic ambiguity remains).
segments.s070-g47: Impersonal prohibition + subjunctive: 'نباید که' = 'it must not / you should not (that...)'. 'چون من' = 'like me'. 'شوم' is first‑person present/subjunctive of شدن ('become'), used here in a subordinate clause introduced by که; the predicate noun 'چاره‌جوی' ('seeker of remedy/help') completes the clause. In context the speaker warns the addressee against becoming a seeker as the speaker was.
segments.s070-g48: Address to the addressee: 'تو' = you; 'رودابه را' is the definite object marked by را (Rudabeh). 'سختی' = 'hardship/severity' is the direct object or effect. The verb position is compressed/archaic: 'آری' here is not the modern word 'yes' and functions as an archaic/poetic verb form (possible senses: 'you give / you put / you make' or an old poetic particle) followed by the prepositional phrase 'به روی' = 'upon the face/in the presence of' — together meaning 'do not put hardship on Rudabeh before/against you' (the exact lexical force of آری is archaic and context-dependent).
segments.s070-g9: مگر at the start introduces a rhetorical or incredulous question (
segments.s070-g10: برآساید is a present/subjunctive‑style pastive form meaning 'be appeased / be relieved' (passive/impersonal sense). The conjunction و links the two predicates. رام گردد is a two‑word verbal phrase: رام (adjective 'tame, pacified') + گردد (present/subjunctive/passive 'become'), literally 'become tame'. زمین is the patient/affected noun 'the land/realm'. Together: '...be appeased and the land become pacified.'
segments.s070-g11: به کابل marks a destination/location 'to Kabul'. که introduces a relative/locative clause 'where/that', and با سام یارد is an accompaniment phrase 'with Sam (and) Yārd' or names in sequence; word order places the companions before the verb. چخید is a past verb meaning 'gave a short sound / cried out / uttered a sharp sound' (here describing an outcry). The clause therefore says 'in Kabul, where Yārd (together with Sam) cried out.'
segments.s070-g12: ازان = از + آن 'from that/thereof' introduces the source. زخم گرزش is a genitive two‑word unit: زخم 'wound' + گرزش (گرز + -ش) here meaning 'a blow from a mace/club' or 'the mace‑blow', so together 'the wound of that mace‑blow'. که introduces the relative clause 'which', یارد is the affected person/name, and چشید (past of چشیدن) is used idiomatically 'to taste/experience (suffering)' — 'which Yārd experienced'. The segment therefore means 'from that wound/the blow of the mace which Yārd had experienced.'
segments.s070-g13: چو (classical) means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بشنید is past tense 'she heard' (simple past). سیندخت is the subject (proper name). بنشست is the past tense 'sat down' or 'sank down'. پست is an adjective/adverb 'low, downcast' modifying how she sat (i.e., she sat down low or in a low/abased way). Together: 'When Sīndokht heard, she sat down low.'
segments.s070-g14: دل چاره‌جوی is a noun phrase 'her heart (دل) — a seeker of remedy (چاره‌جوی)' where چاره‌جوی functions as an appositive/epithet. اندر اندیشه بست is a classical idiom: اندر = 'in/within', اندیشه = 'thought', and بست (from بستن) here means 'bound/fastened' or 'set' (i.e., she 'bent/turned' her heart to thought). So the whole segment says she turned her heart (which sought a remedy) inward into thought.
segments.s070-g15: یکی چاره = 'one/ a single remedy' (یکی modifies چاره). آورد is past 'brought forth / produced'. از دل is an ablative/source phrase 'from the heart' (i.e., from inward thought), and به جای is an adverbial phrase 'to the place / into effect' (meaning she put it forward). The sequence means 'she brought forth one remedy from her heart into effect.'
segments.s070-g16: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بد here is an archaic/poetic intensifier attached to the following adjective (in older Persian 'بد' can function as an intensifier rather than strictly 'bad'); it modifies ژرف‌بین 'deep‑seeing/insightful'. و links the second adjective فزاینده 'increasing/augmenting'. رای means 'counsel/plan' here. The clause characterizes the remedy as 'deeply far‑seeing and having a growing/augmenting counsel (i.e., resourcefully wise).' Note: the intensifying use of بد is a poetic feature.
segments.s070-g33: This is an archaic word order: the experiencer 'مرا' (me) appears before the predicate phrase 'رفت باید' (literally 'going is necessary for me'), so the whole means 'I must go.' 'باید' is an impersonal modal meaning 'it is necessary / I must.' 'به نزدیک سام' is a directional phrase 'to near Sam' (به + محل/adverb).
segments.s070-g34: 'زبان' here means 'speech / utterance' (literally 'tongue') and is the thing the speaker will open; 'برگشایم' is 1st-person singular, present/subjunctive form of an older verb meaning 'I open / I unseal / I reveal' (the final -م marks 1st person). 'چو' introduces a simile 'like'; 'تیغ از نیام' is 'a sword from its scabbard' — i.e., the tongue will be loosed like a sword drawn out.
segments.s070-g35: 'بگویم' is a 1st-person verbal form meaning 'I will say / I shall tell' (subjunctive/form used for future-oriented statement). 'بدو' is the dative 'to him.' 'آنچه گفتن سزد' is a relative clause: 'that which (آنچه) [it] befits to say' — 'گفتن' is the infinitive/gerund 'to say' and 'سزد' (it befits) governs that infinitive.
segments.s070-g36: This is a short clause with subject 'خرد خام' ('raw / immature intellect' — 'خرد' = judgement/wisdom modified by 'خام' = raw) and the verb 'پزد' (3rd-singular present) meaning literally 'cooks / boils.' 'گفتارها را' is the definite plural object 'the words/speeches' with the object marker 'را'. The line uses 'پزد' metaphorically: immature minds 'cook' or mishandle words.
segments.s070-g37: Two parallel prepositional/causal phrases introduced by poetic 'ز' (= از, 'from/because of'): 'ز من رنج جان' = 'because of my life's suffering' and 'ز تو خواسته' = 'because of your desire/wish.' Word order places the genitive/causal phrase before the rest of the clause for emphasis.
segments.s070-g38: 'سپردن' is the infinitive 'to entrust / the entrusting' and here functions as a nominal action: 'the entrusting to me of the adorned treasure.' 'به من' is the dative 'to me.' 'گنج آراسته' is a noun phrase 'the adorned/ornamented treasure' with the adjective after the noun in normal Persian order.
segments.s070-g39: Word order is verb-first (بدو گفت) 'he said to him,' then the speaker's name 'مهراب.' The following phrase 'بستان کلید' in context is an imperative-like instruction 'take the key' (classical/poetic imperative), though the lexicon entry given for 'بستان' is a noun 'garden' — here it functions as an imperative or is a poetic/inverted phrase. 'کلید' is the object 'key.'
segments.s070-g40: 'غم گنج' is a genitive phrase 'the sorrow/grief of the treasure.' 'هرگز نباید کشید' is an impersonal negative construction: 'one must never کشید.' 'نباید' expresses prohibition/necessity ('must not'); 'کشید' here is an older/higher-register infinitive/present meaning 'to draw / to pull out' (i.e., 'draw forth' or 'open'), but the provided sense list does not include that meaning, so it is marked separately.

sentences.s070-s37: Like something heavenly, a single golden throne was set upon it, and upon it several kinds of jewels were placed.
sentences.s070-s38: Its royal span measured twenty widths, and atop it there were three hundred more.
sentences.s070-s39: From those, four Indian elephants bore all the garments and carpets.
sentences.s070-s9: Thereupon he sprang forward, put his hand to his bow, and came upon King Khurshidfash.
sentences.s070-s10: He said to him, 'Hear one word from me: when another desire comes, do it.'
sentences.s070-s11: He seeks you for the sake of the body; forgive him, and know that this night is pregnant with vengeance.
sentences.s070-s12: Even if the night is long, be gone—darkness will not remain long.
sentences.s070-s25: In this world, his life is a sorrow to me; now, with you, his pledge is for a single day.
sentences.s070-s26: I do not have such a sorrow for myself; this pain and grief of mine comes from him.
sentences.s070-s27: At first someone exacted a stern pledge from her; then, like a man, he sought a way of remedy.
sentences.s070-s28: He adorned his body with brocade and gold, and his head with pearls and ruby‑rich ornaments.
sentences.s070-s1: When this story became known in Kabul, the marzban was filled with reproach.
sentences.s070-s2: He flew into a rage and summoned Sindokht; Rudabeh's whole anger he turned upon him.
sentences.s070-s3: He said to him, 'Now there is no counsel except this: I have no standing with the king of the world.'
sentences.s070-s4: That I should drag the body of the impure maiden and make you lament before the assembly.
sentences.s070-s13: When the day grows bright like a clear spring, the world becomes like a gem of Badakhshan.
sentences.s070-s14: Mihrab said to him: 'From of old, do not tell tales among the heroes.'
sentences.s070-s15: Tell what you know, and do your utmost with your life; but if need be, don a cloak of blood upon your body.
sentences.s070-s16: Siandokht said to him: 'O noble one, there is no need that my coat be stained with my blood.'
sentences.s070-s29: Then from his treasure of gold he took out thirty thousand dinars for offerings.
sentences.s070-s30: They brought thirty golden trappings from the Tazi (Arabian) and the Persian steeds.
sentences.s070-s31: One wore a golden collar; the worshipper washed. Each held a golden cup in his hand.
sentences.s070-s32: Full of musk and camphor and ruby and gold, and set with several turquoise gems.
sentences.s070-s33: Forty brocade garments on the body, worked with gold; their design set with jewels of every kind.
sentences.s070-s34: Two hundred Indian swords, golden and silvery; of these thirty were given to Zahrāb Parand.
sentences.s070-s35: One hundred Ashtar, all female and red‑haired; one hundred Astar, all beasts of burden that follow the road.
sentences.s070-s36: One jeweled, majestic crown; a necklace, with attendants, and earrings.
sentences.s070-s21: Bring the worshipper, the steed, the throne and the hat; prepare them and take them with you on the road.
sentences.s070-s22: Unless the city of Kabul burns for us; when it withers, it will kindle for us.
sentences.s070-s23: Thus spoke Sindukht, the famed Kay.
sentences.s070-s24: You should not, like me, become a seeker of remedy; you must not put hardship on Rudabeh with your face.
sentences.s070-s5: Can the King of Iran be relieved of this anger and hatred, and the land be pacified?
sentences.s070-s6: To Kabul — where, with Sam, Yārd uttered a cry — from that wound of the mace which Yārd had suffered.
sentences.s070-s7: When Sīndokht heard, she sank down low; her heart, seeking a remedy, she set into thought.
sentences.s070-s8: She produced one remedy from her heart — a solution that was deeply far‑seeing and rich in increasing counsel.
sentences.s070-s17: I must go near Sam, and I will unloose my tongue like a sword from its scabbard.
sentences.s070-s18: I will tell him whatever ought to be said; crude minds boil (spoil) words.
sentences.s070-s19: Because of my life's suffering and because of your desire, the entrusting to me of the adorned treasure.
sentences.s070-s20: Mehrab said to him, 'Take the key of the garden; the treasure's sorrow must never be drawn.'
